scenario_description: Urban and electronics demand rises sharply, premium transport usage grows, and Q4 strain creates selective service pressure.
Rural customers expand quickly, average distance grows, road dependency rises, and service levels become more volatile.
Management aggressively reduces inventory and safety stock, improving cash efficiency at the cost of fill rate and stockout risk.
The network invests in faster transport and stronger provider performance, producing higher on-time delivery at higher cost.
Rising fuel prices create severe road-cost inflation and compress margins, especially on long-haul shipments.
A network redesign shifts long-haul volume toward rail, reducing cost per kilometer while modestly extending some lead times.
Electronics demand remains healthy, but a quality problem drives return rates sharply higher and erodes contribution margin.
The year-end peak becomes much more extreme, creating forecasting misses, stockouts, and cost escalation in November and December.
Food products get operational priority, improving freshness and service while increasing expedited shipments for selected lanes.
The East warehouse faces upstream disruption, causing longer lead times, lower fill rates, and elevated stockouts.
A process-improvement program in the South warehouse improves service, throughput, and inventory control.
Demand softens and management protects service through higher inventories, creating weaker turnover but lower stockout rates.
The company reduces air usage and moves freight to rail, cutting average cost while stretching some service times.
Both selling prices and input costs increase materially, changing revenue and margin dynamics while demand cools slightly.
The network is rebalanced across providers and warehouses, producing strong service and moderate cost performance.
